title: Suturing — Basic
oneLiner: Clean laceration closure to achieve haemostasis and optimal cosmesis; correct technique minimises infection and scarring.

sections.Killers First — What Will Kill Them In T:
- **Necrotising fasciitis:** Pain out of proportion to appearance, crepitus on palpation, rapid systemic toxicity (tachycardia, fever, altered GCS). Do NOT suture until surgically debrided.
- **Retained foreign body:** Radiopaque (metal, glass) or radiolucent (wood, plastic) — can cause deep abscess, tetanus, or delayed wound dehiscence. **Single finding:** wound overlies a known high-risk area (e.g. glass injury on palm) or you feel a "click" on probing.
sections.Presentation:
- **Cardinal symptoms:** History of sharp trauma (knife, glass, metal), clean wound edges, minimal contamination, wound ≤12 hours old (face ≤24 hours), no active bleeding.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspect wound depth, length, and shape.
  - Assess neurovascular status distally (sensation, motor function, capillary refill).
  - Check for exposed tendon, bone, or joint capsule.
  - Evaluate wound base and contamination (dirt, debris, bite marks).
  - Determine tetanus vaccination status.
  - Document wound location and any surrounding erythema or oedema.
sections.Differential Diagnosis:
- **Clean laceration requiring sutures** vs. **abrasion** (superficial, no full-thickness skin penetration — do not suture, simple dressing).
- **Laceration with tendon/nerve injury** vs. **simple skin-only wound** — test distal function; if deficit, do not close until specialist review.
- **Bite wound (human/animal)** vs. **non-bite wound** — high infection risk; suture only if cosmetically necessary and with prophylactic antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or metronidazole per local policy).
- **Splitter:** A wound that is >12 hours old (except face) or visibly contaminated should be considered for delayed primary closure or healing by secondary intention — **do not suture blindly**.
sections.Investigations:
- **Bedside:** Wound exploration with sterile glove and probe to assess depth, foreign body, and tendon involvement.
- **Bloods:** Not routine unless signs of systemic infection (FBC, CRP, wound swab if purulent).
- **Imaging:** Plain X-ray (two views) if foreign body suspected (glass, metal, gravel) — especially in puncture wounds or over hands/feet.
- **Special tests:** Tetanus immunoglobulin/toxoid status check via patient history or immunisation record.
- **Never-miss test:** **Plain X-ray of the wound area** for any penetrating trauma where glass or metal is possible — omission leads to retained foreign body and chronic sinus.
sections.Management:
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Cleanse wound with sterile saline or warm tap water. Remove all visible debris.
  - Irrigate with high-pressure (20 mL syringe + 18G cannula) saline — at least 100–200 mL for small wounds.
  - Infiltrate local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% plain or with adrenaline [avoid digits, penis, ear tips]) — dose: max 3 mg/kg plain, 7 mg/kg with adrenaline. Wait 2–5 minutes for full effect.
  - Prepare sterile field: gloves, drape, antiseptic (povidone-iodine or chlorhexidine) around wound (not inside).
  - Select suture material: non-absorbable monofilament (nylon or polypropylene) for skin; absorbable (vicryl) for deep dermal/subcuticular.
  - Needle size: 3-0 to 4-0 (trunk/extremities), 5-0 to 6-0 (face), 4-0 to 5-0 (scalp/hands).
  - Technique: simple interrupted sutures — exert wound edges (needle entry at 90°, exit at 90°, equal depth on both sides). Space sutures evenly to avoid dog-ears.
  - Tie knots with instrument tie (surgeon’s knot). Cut tails 3–5 mm (avoid long tails in hair-bearing areas).
  - Apply sterile dressing (non-adherent pad + tape) and give aftercare instructions.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If admitted for other reasons: keep wound dry for 24–48 hours. Change dressing daily if soiled.
  - Observe for signs of infection (erythema, swelling, pus, fever).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Suture removal timing: face 5–7 days, scalp 7–10 days, trunk 10–14 days, extremities 10–14 days, over joints 14 days.
  - Wound care advice: keep clean and dry, no swimming, avoid strenuous activity that stretches wound.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not typically needed unless concurrent orthopaedic/vascular injury. If wound is contaminated, consider prophylactic antibiotics (flucloxacillin or co-amoxiclav per local policy) for 5 days.
sections.Drugs & Doses:
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL) — max dose **plain**: 3 mg/kg (≈ 210 mg for 70 kg adult = 21 mL); **with adrenaline (1:200,000)**: 7 mg/kg (≈ 490 mg = 49 mL). Use aspirating syringe to avoid intravascular injection.
- **Alternative:** Bupivacaine 0.25–0.5% (longer acting) — max dose 2 mg/kg (≈ 140 mg for 70 kg). [Check local policy for specific concentrations and volumes.]
- **Tetanus prophylaxis:**
  - Clean, minor wound → tetanus toxoid (if >10 years since last dose or unknown).
  - Dirty/contaminated wound → tetanus toxoid + immune globulin (if >5 years since last dose or unknown). Dose: [check local policy – typically 250 IU IM for adults].
- **Antibiotics (if indicated — bite, heavily contaminated, or immunocompromised):**
  - Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS for 5 days (or clindamycin 300–450 mg PO QDS if penicillin-allergic).
  - Prophylaxis only for high-risk wounds, not clean lacerations.
- **Key contraindications:** Lidocaine with adrenaline contraindicated in digital, penile, or ear tip blocks (risk of ischaemia).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Exceeding maximum lidocaine dose — presents with perioral tingling, metallic taste, seizures, then cardiac arrest. Always calculate weight-based dose and monitor for toxicity. If using multiple syringes, track total volume given.
sections.Complications:
- **Acute:**
  - Wound infection (erythema, purulent discharge, spreading cellulitis) — remove sutures if infected, start antibiotics, culture.
  - Haematoma or seroma — evacuate if tense; may require drainage.
  - Dehiscence (wound opening) — early (days) due to tension or infection; late (weeks) from poor technique or underlying disease.
- **Chronic:**
  - Hypertrophic scar or keloid — more common in darker skin types and over sternum, shoulders, earlobes. Refer to dermatology for steroid injection or silicone gel.
  - Suture track marks — caused by leaving sutures too long or tying too tight (strangulation).
sections.Prognosis: - Clean, well-approximated wounds heal with minimal scarring in 7–14 days. Infection risk is <5% for clean lacerations if proper technique used. Facial wounds usually heal with excellent cosmesis. Contaminated or bite wounds have higher infection (up to 30%) and require close follow-up.
sections.Pearls (positive actions):
- **Always evert wound edges** — slight outward turning ensures flat scar. Use vertical or horizontal mattress sutures if edges tend to invert (e.g., over joints, thin skin).
- **Use the smallest needle and fewest sutures possible** — reduces foreign body reaction and suture marks. Deep dermal sutures (absorbable) relieve skin tension for larger wounds.
- **Check neurovascular function before AND after anaesthesia** — document distal sensation and motor function. If deficit after anaesthesia, suspect nerve block or compartment syndrome from over-injection.
sections.Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid):
- **Never close a dirty, devitalised, or infected wound** — delayed primary closure (3–5 days) or healing by secondary intention is safer.
- **Do not use excessive tension** — sutures should just appose edges, not blanch them. Strangulation causes ischaemia and poor healing.
- **Avoid leaving suture tails longer than 5 mm on the face** — they become uncomfortable and trap debris. On scalp, burry tails to avoid tangling hair.
sections.Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home):
- **Return if:**
  - Redness spreading >2 cm from wound, increasing pain, pus, or fever (clinical infection).
  - Wound gapes open or bleeds through dressing.
  - Numbness or weakness distal to the wound (nerve injury or compartment syndrome).
- **Follow-up:** Wound check in 48–72 hours in primary care or practice nurse clinic. Suture removal as per timing above.
sections.Escalation Criteria:
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Wound involves exposed bone, joint, tendon, or major nerve (requires ortho/plastics referral in theatre).
  - Uncontrollable bleeding despite pressure and haemostatics (suspect arterial injury).
  - Signs of necrotising fasciitis: pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, rapidly spreading erythema, systemic toxicity (NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2, hypotension). **Immediate surgical debridement — do not close.**
- **ICU/crash team if:** Anaphylaxis to local anaesthetic, lidocaine toxicity with seizures or arrhythmia, or haemodynamic instability from sepsis.

rawMarkdown:
## Suturing — Basic
**One-liner:** Clean laceration closure to achieve haemostasis and optimal cosmesis; correct technique minimises infection and scarring.

### Killers First — What Will Kill Them In The First Hour?
- **Necrotising fasciitis:** Pain out of proportion to appearance, crepitus on palpation, rapid systemic toxicity (tachycardia, fever, altered GCS). Do NOT suture until surgically debrided.
- **Retained foreign body:** Radiopaque (metal, glass) or radiolucent (wood, plastic) — can cause deep abscess, tetanus, or delayed wound dehiscence. **Single finding:** wound overlies a known high-risk area (e.g. glass injury on palm) or you feel a "click" on probing.

### Presentation
- **Cardinal symptoms:** History of sharp trauma (knife, glass, metal), clean wound edges, minimal contamination, wound ≤12 hours old (face ≤24 hours), no active bleeding.
- **Key exam findings:**
  - Inspect wound depth, length, and shape.
  - Assess neurovascular status distally (sensation, motor function, capillary refill).
  - Check for exposed tendon, bone, or joint capsule.
  - Evaluate wound base and contamination (dirt, debris, bite marks).
  - Determine tetanus vaccination status.
  - Document wound location and any surrounding erythema or oedema.

### Differential Diagnosis
- **Clean laceration requiring sutures** vs. **abrasion** (superficial, no full-thickness skin penetration — do not suture, simple dressing).
- **Laceration with tendon/nerve injury** vs. **simple skin-only wound** — test distal function; if deficit, do not close until specialist review.
- **Bite wound (human/animal)** vs. **non-bite wound** — high infection risk; suture only if cosmetically necessary and with prophylactic antibiotics (co-amoxiclav or metronidazole per local policy).
- **Splitter:** A wound that is >12 hours old (except face) or visibly contaminated should be considered for delayed primary closure or healing by secondary intention — **do not suture blindly**.

### Investigations
- **Bedside:** Wound exploration with sterile glove and probe to assess depth, foreign body, and tendon involvement.
- **Bloods:** Not routine unless signs of systemic infection (FBC, CRP, wound swab if purulent).
- **Imaging:** Plain X-ray (two views) if foreign body suspected (glass, metal, gravel) — especially in puncture wounds or over hands/feet.
- **Special tests:** Tetanus immunoglobulin/toxoid status check via patient history or immunisation record.
- **Never-miss test:** **Plain X-ray of the wound area** for any penetrating trauma where glass or metal is possible — omission leads to retained foreign body and chronic sinus.

### Management
- **Immediate (first hour / ED):**
  - Cleanse wound with sterile saline or warm tap water. Remove all visible debris.
  - Irrigate with high-pressure (20 mL syringe + 18G cannula) saline — at least 100–200 mL for small wounds.
  - Infiltrate local anaesthetic (lidocaine 1% plain or with adrenaline [avoid digits, penis, ear tips]) — dose: max 3 mg/kg plain, 7 mg/kg with adrenaline. Wait 2–5 minutes for full effect.
  - Prepare sterile field: gloves, drape, antiseptic (povidone-iodine or chlorhexidine) around wound (not inside).
  - Select suture material: non-absorbable monofilament (nylon or polypropylene) for skin; absorbable (vicryl) for deep dermal/subcuticular.
  - Needle size: 3-0 to 4-0 (trunk/extremities), 5-0 to 6-0 (face), 4-0 to 5-0 (scalp/hands).
  - Technique: simple interrupted sutures — exert wound edges (needle entry at 90°, exit at 90°, equal depth on both sides). Space sutures evenly to avoid dog-ears.
  - Tie knots with instrument tie (surgeon’s knot). Cut tails 3–5 mm (avoid long tails in hair-bearing areas).
  - Apply sterile dressing (non-adherent pad + tape) and give aftercare instructions.
- **Ward (first 24–72h):**
  - If admitted for other reasons: keep wound dry for 24–48 hours. Change dressing daily if soiled.
  - Observe for signs of infection (erythema, swelling, pus, fever).
- **Long-term / outpatient:**
  - Suture removal timing: face 5–7 days, scalp 7–10 days, trunk 10–14 days, extremities 10–14 days, over joints 14 days.
  - Wound care advice: keep clean and dry, no swimming, avoid strenuous activity that stretches wound.
- **Default Orders (if admitted):** Not typically needed unless concurrent orthopaedic/vascular injury. If wound is contaminated, consider prophylactic antibiotics (flucloxacillin or co-amoxiclav per local policy) for 5 days.

### Drugs & Doses
- **Local anaesthetic:** Lidocaine 1% (10 mg/mL) — max dose **plain**: 3 mg/kg (≈ 210 mg for 70 kg adult = 21 mL); **with adrenaline (1:200,000)**: 7 mg/kg (≈ 490 mg = 49 mL). Use aspirating syringe to avoid intravascular injection.
- **Alternative:** Bupivacaine 0.25–0.5% (longer acting) — max dose 2 mg/kg (≈ 140 mg for 70 kg). [Check local policy for specific concentrations and volumes.]
- **Tetanus prophylaxis:**
  - Clean, minor wound → tetanus toxoid (if >10 years since last dose or unknown).
  - Dirty/contaminated wound → tetanus toxoid + immune globulin (if >5 years since last dose or unknown). Dose: [check local policy – typically 250 IU IM for adults].
- **Antibiotics (if indicated — bite, heavily contaminated, or immunocompromised):**
  - Co-amoxiclav 625 mg PO TDS for 5 days (or clindamycin 300–450 mg PO QDS if penicillin-allergic).
  - Prophylaxis only for high-risk wounds, not clean lacerations.
- **Key contraindications:** Lidocaine with adrenaline contraindicated in digital, penile, or ear tip blocks (risk of ischaemia).
- **Most dangerous dose error:** Exceeding maximum lidocaine dose — presents with perioral tingling, metallic taste, seizures, then cardiac arrest. Always calculate weight-based dose and monitor for toxicity. If using multiple syringes, track total volume given.

### Complications
- **Acute:**
  - Wound infection (erythema, purulent discharge, spreading cellulitis) — remove sutures if infected, start antibiotics, culture.
  - Haematoma or seroma — evacuate if tense; may require drainage.
  - Dehiscence (wound opening) — early (days) due to tension or infection; late (weeks) from poor technique or underlying disease.
- **Chronic:**
  - Hypertrophic scar or keloid — more common in darker skin types and over sternum, shoulders, earlobes. Refer to dermatology for steroid injection or silicone gel.
  - Suture track marks — caused by leaving sutures too long or tying too tight (strangulation).

### Prognosis
- Clean, well-approximated wounds heal with minimal scarring in 7–14 days. Infection risk is <5% for clean lacerations if proper technique used. Facial wounds usually heal with excellent cosmesis. Contaminated or bite wounds have higher infection (up to 30%) and require close follow-up.

### Pearls (positive actions)
- **Always evert wound edges** — slight outward turning ensures flat scar. Use vertical or horizontal mattress sutures if edges tend to invert (e.g., over joints, thin skin).
- **Use the smallest needle and fewest sutures possible** — reduces foreign body reaction and suture marks. Deep dermal sutures (absorbable) relieve skin tension for larger wounds.
- **Check neurovascular function before AND after anaesthesia** — document distal sensation and motor function. If deficit after anaesthesia, suspect nerve block or compartment syndrome from over-injection.

### Pitfalls (Errors To Avoid)
- **Never close a dirty, devitalised, or infected wound** — delayed primary closure (3–5 days) or healing by secondary intention is safer.
- **Do not use excessive tension** — sutures should just appose edges, not blanch them. Strangulation causes ischaemia and poor healing.
- **Avoid leaving suture tails longer than 5 mm on the face** — they become uncomfortable and trap debris. On scalp, burry tails to avoid tangling hair.

### Safety Netting (If Patient Goes Home)
- **Return if:**
  - Redness spreading >2 cm from wound, increasing pain, pus, or fever (clinical infection).
  - Wound gapes open or bleeds through dressing.
  - Numbness or weakness distal to the wound (nerve injury or compartment syndrome).
- **Follow-up:** Wound check in 48–72 hours in primary care or practice nurse clinic. Suture removal as per timing above.

### Escalation Criteria
- **Call senior (registrar/consultant) if:**
  - Wound involves exposed bone, joint, tendon, or major nerve (requires ortho/plastics referral in theatre).
  - Uncontrollable bleeding despite pressure and haemostatics (suspect arterial injury).
  - Signs of necrotising fasciitis: pain out of proportion, crepitus, bullae, rapidly spreading erythema, systemic toxicity (NEWS2 ≥5, lactate >2, hypotension). **Immediate surgical debridement — do not close.**
- **ICU/crash team if:** Anaphylaxis to local anaesthetic, lidocaine toxicity with seizures or arrhythmia, or haemodynamic instability from sepsis.
